RX J1856.5-3754.notes: The closest known neutron star to Sol and the brightest member of the Magnificent Seven — a group of isolated, radio-quiet neutron stars discovered in X-rays. Surface temperature ~700,000 K. Its unusual smoothness has made it a candidate for exotic interior physics like a quark star.

RX J0720.4-3125.notes: Magnificent Seven isolated neutron star. Shows long-term variability in its X-ray spectrum and pulse profile, attributed to precession of a misaligned rotation and magnetic axis.

RX J0806.4-4123.notes: Magnificent Seven isolated neutron star. Slow rotator with an 11.37-second spin period.

RX J0420.0-5022.notes: Magnificent Seven isolated neutron star. Faintest of the seven in the X-ray band.

RX J1605.3+3249.notes: Magnificent Seven isolated neutron star. No confirmed pulsation period, suggesting an axisymmetric magnetic field or near-alignment of the spin and magnetic axes.

RX J2143.0+0654.notes: Magnificent Seven isolated neutron star. Shows a broad X-ray absorption feature often interpreted as a proton cyclotron line from a ~10^14 G magnetic field.

RX J1308.6+2127.notes: Magnificent Seven isolated neutron star. Second-strongest magnetic field of the group; 10.31-second spin period.

PSR J0437-4715.notes: The closest and brightest millisecond pulsar — spins 173.7 times per second. Orbits a white dwarf companion every 5.74 days. NICER measurements have constrained its mass and radius to unprecedented precision.

Geminga.notes: The second-brightest persistent gamma-ray source in the sky, yet invisible at radio wavelengths. A ~340,000-year-old pulsar moving at 211 km/s with a visible X-ray bow shock, likely the remnant of a nearby supernova that helped excavate the Local Bubble.

PSR J0108-1431.notes: Among the oldest, faintest and closest known radio pulsars. Age ~166 million years; just past the pulsar 'death line' where radio emission is expected to shut off.

PSR B0950+08.notes: One of the earliest discovered pulsars (1968), with a 253 ms period. Exceptionally wide interpulse emission — the beam appears to cover most of the sky.

Vela Pulsar.notes: The brightest persistent pulsar in gamma-rays, embedded in the Vela supernova remnant. Rotates ~11 times per second. Its occasional 'glitches' — sudden spin-ups — are a key probe of the superfluid interior of neutron stars.
